finding.summary: The axi_lite_interface module, instantiated inside dma_wrapper for DMA register access, performs no privilege, lock, or access-control check on incoming AXI requests. The acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl_i inputs received by dma_wrapper are never forwarded into or checked within the axi_lite_interface, meaning any AXI master can read or write DMA configuration registers (source/destination addresses, start, length) without restriction.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates dma_wrapper and connects acct_ctrl_i from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8] and reglk_ctrl_i from reglk_ctrl[8*8+7:8*8], indicating that privilege-level-based access control signals and register-lock controls are provided at the wrapper boundary.
evidence.supports_claim: Access control inputs exist at the top level and are passed to dma_wrapper, establishing that a guard is intended.

evidence.description: dma_wrapper declares acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl_i as inputs (lines 46-47) and declares en_acct as a separate signal (line 67) alongside en, but the axi_lite_interface submodule is instantiated (lines 79-84) with en_acct connected to en_o from axi_lite_interface. There is no source-code evidence that acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i gates, masks, or qualifies the en, we, address, or wdata signals before they reach DMA registers. The guard signals are received but their enforcement path into the AXI interface is absent in the provided code.
evidence.supports_claim: The permission/lock inputs are declared but not shown to gate the AXI interface's enable or write signals, indicating a missing guard.

evidence.description: axi_lite_interface contains no privilege check, no lock check, and no access-control logic of any kind. It unconditionally asserts en_o=1, we_o=1, and address_o from AXI request fields whenever the state machine transitions to READ or WRITE states (lines 103, 119-120). There is no input port for acct_ctrl or reglk signals, and no conditional guard before asserting these outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: Any AXI request reaching axi_lite_interface will directly enable read or write to DMA registers with no permission verification.

evidence.description: DMA configuration registers (start_reg, length_reg, source_addr_lsb_reg, source_addr_msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb_reg, dest_addr_msb_reg, core_lock_reg) are the targets of the unguarded AXI write path. Unauthorized writes to these registers can redirect DMA transfers to arbitrary memory locations.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive DMA control registers are accessible via the unguarded AXI path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dma_wrapper receives both an access-control signal (acct_ctrl_i) and a register-lock signal (reglk_ctrl_i) from the top level, indicating that hardware-enforced access control is intended for the DMA peripheral. However, the axi_lite_interface submodule instantiated inside dma_wrapper has no port for these signals and no internal guard logic. The source snippets show axi_lite_interface unconditionally enabling reads and writes based purely on AXI handshake signals, with no privilege or lock check. While en_acct is declared as a signal in dma_wrapper (presumably to carry a gated version of en_o), the actual gating logic using acct_ctrl_i is not present in the provided source, constituting a missing guard over the DMA register access path.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI master (e.g., a user-mode software agent that can inject AXI transactions) can read or write DMA configuration registers without privilege enforcement. This enables redirection of DMA transfers to or from arbitrary physical memory addresses, bypassing memory isolation and potentially allowing privilege escalation, data exfiltration, or corruption of protected memory regions.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full body of dma_wrapper.sv beyond line 84 is not provided; it is possible that en_acct is computed as (en & acct_ctrl_i) and used instead of en in subsequent register-write logic. If such gating exists in the unshown portion, the violation would be partially mitigated for the enable signal, though the address and wdata paths would still need scrutiny. The reglk_ctrl_i enforcement path is also not visible.

finding.summary: The DMA wrapper's AXI read path returns the value of core_lock_reg (a security-sensitive lock register) unconditionally to any AXI reader, with no privilege or lock-enforcement guard on the read side.

evidence.description: The read-data multiplexer at lines 162-184 maps address offset 7 directly to core_lock_reg without checking acct_ctrl_i, reglk_ctrl_i, or any privilege level. Any AXI master that can issue a read to the DMA register block receives the lock register value.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive register (core_lock_reg) is readable by any AXI master without an access-control or privilege guard on the read path.

evidence.description: The module declares reglk_ctrl_i (register lock control) and acct_ctrl_i (account/privilege control) as inputs, indicating that access-control signals are available, but the read-side always block (SNIP-0004) never consults them.
evidence.supports_claim: Access-control inputs exist but are not applied to the read data path, leaving the protection mechanism absent for reads.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals connects reglk_ctrl[8*8+7:8*8] and acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8] to the DMA wrapper, confirming that privilege and lock signals are driven to the module from a higher-level security context, yet they are ignored inside the read multiplexer.
evidence.supports_claim: The system provides privilege-level and register-lock signals that are supposed to gate DMA register access, but those signals are not used in the read path.

evidence.description: axi_resp_o.r.data is assigned directly from data_i (line 42), which is driven by rdata from the DMA wrapper. The axi_lite_interface itself performs no filtering; whatever rdata the wrapper puts out is forwarded verbatim to the AXI read channel.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI interface transparently exposes whatever data the DMA wrapper provides, so a missing guard in the DMA read mux directly leaks sensitive register contents to the bus.

finding.reasoning_summary: The DMA wrapper declares security-control inputs (reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, pmpcfg_i) but its combinational read-data multiplexer (always @(*) block, lines 157-186) selects register contents—including the security-sensitive core_lock_reg—based only on the decoded address and en signal. None of the access-control or privilege signals gate the read path. The axi_lite_interface passes the resulting rdata straight to axi_resp_o.r.data without any interposition. Consequently, any AXI master that can address the DMA peripheral can read core_lock_reg (and all other DMA registers) regardless of privilege level or lock state.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI master can read core_lock_reg and all other DMA control registers (source/destination addresses, length, start, done, end, valid) without authorization. This enables information disclosure of lock state, DMA transfer parameters, and potentially sensitive memory addresses, and may allow an attacker to infer or manipulate DMA security state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The write path (not fully shown in snippets) may or may not enforce the lock/privilege checks; the finding here is limited to the confirmed absence of guards on the read path. It is also unknown whether a bus-level firewall upstream of the DMA wrapper provides compensating controls.

finding.summary: The DMA control enable signal (dma_ctrl_en) and associated DMA state registers lack protection against unauthorized writes: the dma_wrapper passes register inputs directly to the dma module without enforcing the core_lock_reg or acct_ctrl_i access-control signals as guards on write operations to DMA configuration registers, allowing unprivileged software to reprogram DMA source/destination addresses and trigger transfers that bypass PMP enforcement.

evidence.description: dma_wrapper declares core_lock_reg (lock) and acct_ctrl_i (account/privilege control) as internal/input signals, but these are never shown to gate the we or en signals that flow to the register-write logic for start_reg, length_reg, source/dest address regs, etc.
evidence.supports_claim: Lock and privilege signals exist but are not connected as guards on register write paths.

evidence.description: The DMA module is instantiated with rst_ni connected as (rst_ni && ~rst_8), and start_reg, length_reg, source_addr_lsb_reg, source_addr_msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb_reg, dest_addr_msb_reg are passed directly as inputs. There is no conditional gating by core_lock_reg or acct_ctrl_i before the values reach the dma module.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: reglk_ctrl_i/acct_ctrl_i/core_lock_reg are not used to filter register writes before they reach the DMA core.

evidence.description: The dma module accepts start_i, length_i, source_addr_lsb_i, source_addr_msb_i, dest_addr_lsb_i, dest_addr_msb_i directly as inputs, latching them unconditionally when dma_ctrl_reg == CTRL_IDLE or CTRL_DONE. No privilege check occurs inside the dma module itself on these inputs.
evidence.supports_claim: DMA transfer configuration registers are latched without any privilege/lock check inside the DMA core.

evidence.description: In CTRL_CHECK_LOAD, pmp_allow_new is set to '(pmp_allow_reg && (pmp_data_allow || (pmp_check_ctr_reg == length_d))) || (we_flag && length_d == 3)'. The we_flag bypass: when we_flag is asserted and length_d==3, PMP permission is unconditionally granted regardless of actual PMP check results, bypassing the memory protection check.
evidence.supports_claim: The we_flag condition creates a PMP bypass path that can allow DMA transfers to unauthorized memory regions.

evidence.description: dma_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i connected, but as shown in SNIP-0009 and SNIP-0010, these inputs are not used to gate register writes or DMA activation.
evidence.supports_claim: Access control inputs are present at the instantiation boundary but are unused within the module, making the protection ineffective.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni deasserted), dma_ctrl_reg, pmp_allow_reg, valid_reg, etc. are cleared to safe values using blocking assignments inside an always_ff block, which is a coding style inconsistency (blocking vs. non-blocking), but more importantly, pmp_allow_reg resets to 0 which is safe. However, since the we_flag bypass exists in combinational logic, reset does not prevent the bypass after reset.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset behavior is correct for initial state but does not mitigate the combinational PMP bypass via we_flag.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dma_wrapper module accepts reglk_ctrl_i (register lock) and acct_ctrl_i (account/privilege control) inputs but never uses them to gate the write-enable or data signals flowing into the DMA configuration registers (start_reg, length_reg, source/dest address registers). These registers are then fed directly into the dma module without privilege checks. Additionally, inside the dma module's CTRL_CHECK_LOAD state, there is an explicit bypass of the PMP check when we_flag is asserted and length_d equals 3, unconditionally granting pmp_allow_new=1. This means a software attacker who can set we_flag and control length_d can bypass the PMP memory protection entirely and trigger DMA transfers to/from arbitrary memory addresses.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software process could program the DMA with arbitrary source/destination addresses and trigger memory transfers that bypass PMP protection, enabling read or write access to privileged memory regions, potentially leading to privilege escalation, data exfiltration, or memory corruption of security-critical data.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full register-write arbitration logic inside dma_wrapper (where core_lock_reg and acct_ctrl_i would ideally be used) is not shown in the provided snippets—only declarations are visible. However, the instantiation in SNIP-0010 confirms that configuration registers are passed directly to the DMA without interposing guards, and SNIP-0006 provides concrete evidence of the PMP bypass via we_flag.

finding.summary: DMA PMP permission check contains a hardcoded bypass condition: when `we_flag` is set and `length_d == 3`, PMP access is unconditionally allowed regardless of actual PMP check results, enabling a privileged software attacker to bypass memory protection on DMA load operations.

evidence.description: Line 216 computes `pmp_allow_new` as: `(pmp_allow_reg && (pmp_data_allow || (pmp_check_ctr_reg == length_d))) || (we_flag && length_d == 3)`. The second disjunct `(we_flag && length_d == 3)` unconditionally sets `pmp_allow_new = 1` when the write-enable flag is set and the DMA length equals 3, completely bypassing the PMP hardware check (`pmp_data_allow`) for the CTRL_CHECK_LOAD state. This means DMA read accesses to otherwise-protected memory regions are permitted if these two conditions hold.
evidence.supports_claim: PMP enforcement for DMA source address reads can be bypassed by setting we_flag=1 and programming length=3, regardless of whether the PMP hardware reports the access as allowed.

evidence.description: `we_flag` is an input logic port of `dma_wrapper` (line 51), connected directly from an external signal into the DMA core. It is not gated by any privilege or lock check before being passed to the DMA state machine.
evidence.supports_claim: The bypass-enabling signal `we_flag` is externally controllable and flows unguarded into the PMP permission computation.

evidence.description: The DMA module is instantiated at line 196 with `rst_ni = rst_ni && ~rst_8`. The `we_flag` input (edge:connects:32) is passed directly to the DMA core without any privilege or lock filtering visible in this instantiation block.
evidence.supports_claim: No guard on `we_flag` is applied at the instantiation boundary between dma_wrapper and the dma module.

evidence.description: The `pmp_allow_reg` is initialized to 0 on reset (line 145) and updated via `pmp_allow_new` computed in the combinational block. The reset correctly clears PMP state, but the combinational bypass in SNIP-0006 can set `pmp_allow_new=1` through the `we_flag && length_d==3` path, defeating the PMP check.
evidence.supports_claim: PMP allow register can be set to 1 via the bypass condition, causing subsequent DMA state machine transitions to proceed as if PMP access is granted.

evidence.description: The DMA module performs AXI memory reads and writes based on source and destination addresses programmed by software. PMP is intended to restrict which memory regions the DMA may access. If PMP is bypassed, the DMA can read from or write to any physical memory address.
evidence.supports_claim: DMA memory transfers to/from arbitrary addresses are the resource protected by the PMP check that can be bypassed.

finding.reasoning_summary: In the CTRL_CHECK_LOAD state of the DMA controller, the computation of `pmp_allow_new` (the signal that accumulates whether PMP checks pass for source addresses) contains an explicit OR branch: `(we_flag && length_d == 3)`. This branch, when true, sets `pmp_allow_new=1` independent of `pmp_data_allow` (the actual PMP hardware output). Since `we_flag` is an unguarded external input and `length_d` is a software-programmable register, an attacker with register-write access can trigger this path to allow DMA reads from PMP-protected memory regions. The bypass is asymmetric: it only appears in CTRL_CHECK_LOAD (reads), not CTRL_CHECK_STORE. The `we_flag` signal is passed from `dma_wrapper` to `dma` without privilege filtering.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can write DMA configuration registers (length=3, we_flag=1) can cause the DMA to read from any physical memory address, bypassing PMP-enforced memory isolation. This enables exfiltration of secrets from protected memory regions (e.g., secure enclaves, kernel memory) by DMA into an attacker-accessible destination buffer.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact mechanism by which `we_flag` is driven externally (e.g., from a privilege-level-controlled register or from untrusted software) is not fully shown in the snippets. However, the bypass condition in source code is unambiguous, and `we_flag` enters the DMA wrapper as an unguarded port input with no visible privilege check in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: DMA PMP permission bypass via we_flag: the pmp_allow_new computation in CTRL_CHECK_LOAD unconditionally grants permission when we_flag is set and length_d==3, bypassing PMP access checks entirely

evidence.description: At line 216, pmp_allow_new is assigned as: (pmp_allow_reg && (pmp_data_allow || (pmp_check_ctr_reg == length_d))) || (we_flag && length_d == 3). The second disjunct '(we_flag && length_d == 3)' unconditionally forces pmp_allow_new to 1 whenever we_flag is asserted and length_d equals 3, regardless of the PMP hardware check result stored in pmp_data_allow. This completely bypasses the PMP read-permission enforcement for the source address range.
evidence.supports_claim: PMP access control can be bypassed when we_flag is asserted and transfer length is 3

evidence.description: we_flag is an external input port of dma_wrapper (line 51), driven from riscv_peripherals via we_flag_3. It is passed directly to the dma module without any privilege-level gating or lock check in the path visible in the snippets.
evidence.supports_claim: we_flag is an externally controllable input that feeds the bypass condition

evidence.description: In riscv_peripherals.sv the dma_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i parameters, but we_flag_3 is connected directly as we_flag without evidence that it is gated by those lock or account-control signals before reaching the PMP bypass logic.
evidence.supports_claim: External caller can supply we_flag to trigger the bypass

evidence.description: On reset, pmp_allow_reg is initialized to 0 and pmp_access_type_reg to ACCESS_READ (line 144-145). The PMP enforcement path through CTRL_CHECK_LOAD is designed to accumulate permission via pmp_data_allow, but the bypass in line 216 short-circuits this accumulation.
evidence.supports_claim: The intended permission state can be overridden by the bypass condition

evidence.description: The outcome of pmp_allow_reg at the end of CTRL_CHECK_LOAD determines whether the DMA proceeds to CTRL_LOAD (line 207: dma_ctrl_new = (pmp_allow_reg && pmp_data_allow) ? CTRL_LOAD : CTRL_CHECK_STORE). If the bypass inflates pmp_allow_reg to 1, the DMA will perform an unauthorized AXI read from the source address.
evidence.supports_claim: Bypassing PMP allows unauthorized DMA read transfers to proceed

finding.reasoning_summary: The CTRL_CHECK_LOAD state iterates over source addresses to verify PMP read permissions. The pmp_allow_new update at dma.sv line 216 contains a special case '(we_flag && length_d == 3)' that forces the permission accumulator to 1 independent of the hardware PMP check (pmp_data_allow). Since we_flag is an external input with no observed privilege gating in the snippets, any caller that can assert we_flag with a 3-word transfer length can bypass PMP source-address checks and cause the DMA to read from otherwise forbidden memory regions.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged software core that can set we_flag=1 and program length_d=3 can cause the DMA to read from any physical address that would normally be blocked by PMP, enabling exfiltration of protected memory contents.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full dma_wrapper register-write logic determining how we_flag_3 is driven in riscv_peripherals is not fully shown; it is possible that we_flag is restricted to privileged contexts at the caller side, but no such restriction is visible in the provided snippets. The CTRL_CHECK_STORE path does not show the same bypass, so the vulnerability may be limited to read (source) PMP checks.

finding.summary: The DMA PMP permission check contains a bypass condition that allows DMA transfers to proceed without PMP authorization when we_flag is set and length_d equals 3, unconditionally granting pmp_allow regardless of actual PMP policy.

evidence.description: In the CTRL_CHECK_LOAD state, pmp_allow_new is computed as: `(pmp_allow_reg && (pmp_data_allow || (pmp_check_ctr_reg == length_d))) || (we_flag && length_d == 3)`. The second disjunct `(we_flag && length_d == 3)` unconditionally sets pmp_allow_new to 1 whenever we_flag is asserted and length_d equals 3, completely bypassing the PMP check (pmp_data_allow) for the load address range.
evidence.supports_claim: PMP access control for DMA read operations can be bypassed when we_flag=1 and length_d=3, allowing unauthorized memory reads without PMP permission.

evidence.description: we_flag is an input port of dma_wrapper (line 51) driven from external logic, and is passed directly into the dma module (edge:connects:32). It is not gated by any privilege or lock check before reaching the PMP bypass logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The we_flag input that triggers the PMP bypass is externally controllable and not protected by any access-control guard within dma_wrapper.

evidence.description: length_i (line 55) and we_flag (line 64) are direct inputs to the dma module. The PMP infrastructure (pmp_allow_reg, pmp_data_allow) is meant to gate DMA transfers, but the bypass condition uses these unguarded inputs to override PMP results.
evidence.supports_claim: DMA transfer parameters including length_i feed the bypass condition directly, making the bypass exploitable through normal register writes.

evidence.description: After CTRL_CHECK_LOAD, if pmp_allow_reg is set (potentially via bypass), CTRL_LOAD is entered (line 207 condition `pmp_allow_reg && pmp_data_allow`). However in CTRL_CHECK_LOAD line 207 shows `(pmp_allow_reg && pmp_data_allow) ? CTRL_LOAD : CTRL_CHECK_STORE`, so CTRL_LOAD requires both. But the bypass sets pmp_allow_new=1 during intermediate check cycles, which accumulates into pmp_allow_reg, potentially satisfying the condition in combination with a single legitimate PMP-allowed address.
evidence.supports_claim: The accumulated pmp_allow_reg value from the bypass can influence the final transition to CTRL_LOAD, enabling DMA reads over memory ranges not fully authorized by PMP.

evidence.description: The dma_wrapper is instantiated with acct_ctrl_i=acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8] and reglk_ctrl_i=reglk_ctrl[8*8+7:8*8], showing account and register-lock controls exist at the wrapper level. However these controls do not prevent the internal PMP bypass logic from operating when we_flag and length_d conditions are met.
evidence.supports_claim: External access controls at the wrapper instantiation level do not close the PMP bypass path inside the dma module.

finding.reasoning_summary: The dma module's CTRL_CHECK_LOAD state machine uses a PMP permission accumulator (pmp_allow_reg) to determine whether a DMA read is authorized. A bypass condition `(we_flag && length_d == 3)` can unconditionally assert pmp_allow_new=1 during check iterations regardless of actual PMP policy (pmp_data_allow). Since we_flag is an unguarded external input passed through dma_wrapper without privilege filtering, an attacker who can write the DMA control registers with we_flag asserted and length_d=3 can cause the PMP load check to pass, potentially enabling unauthorized DMA reads from PMP-protected memory regions.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with access to DMA configuration registers can bypass PMP-enforced read access controls on memory by setting we_flag=1 and length_d=3, causing the DMA controller to proceed with memory read operations (CTRL_LOAD) over regions that PMP would otherwise deny, leading to unauthorized data exfiltration from protected memory.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of we_flag's origin and whether it is privilege-gated upstream of dma_wrapper is not fully visible in the provided snippets. Additionally, the final transition condition to CTRL_LOAD requires both pmp_allow_reg and pmp_data_allow, so the bypass alone may not be sufficient without at least one PMP-allowed address in the range. The pmp_check_ctr decrement logic (CTRL_CHECK_STORE shown, not CTRL_CHECK_LOAD decrement) was not fully visible, introducing some uncertainty about exact iteration behavior.

finding.summary: The DMA module's pmp_access_type_reg is reset to riscv::ACCESS_READ on reset rather than a safe/neutral value, and the reset logic uses blocking assignments inside an always_ff block. More critically, the dma_wrapper resets the DMA sub-module using 'rst_ni && ~rst_8', meaning a secondary reset signal (rst_8) can independently reset the DMA's PMP permission state without resetting the wrapper's register state, creating a potential desynchronization of permission state.

evidence.description: In the reg_update always block (lines 138-164), blocking assignments (=) are used inside an always_ff block during the reset branch (lines 142-147). Specifically, pmp_access_type_reg is initialized to riscv::ACCESS_READ on reset (line 144). Using blocking assignments in always_ff for reset state is a design flaw that can cause race conditions or simulation-synthesis mismatches. Additionally, pmp_allow_reg is reset to 0 (deny), but pmp_access_type_reg is reset to ACCESS_READ which presupposes a specific PMP check type on every reset.
evidence.supports_claim: Blocking assignments in clocked always block for security-critical PMP permission registers (pmp_access_type_reg, pmp_allow_reg, etc.) can cause incorrect protection state during reset.

evidence.description: The DMA module is instantiated with rst_ni = 'rst_ni && ~rst_8' (line 196). This means rst_8 alone can trigger a reset of the DMA's internal PMP permission registers (pmp_access_type_reg, pmp_allow_reg, pmp_check_ctr_reg, valid_reg) independently of the wrapper's own register state (start_reg, length_reg, source/dest addresses). After rst_8 asserts and deasserts, the DMA's PMP state is cleared but the wrapper's address/length registers retain their previous values, allowing a new DMA transfer to be initiated with stale (potentially attacker-controlled) addresses without a fresh PMP check sequence.
evidence.supports_claim: A secondary reset path (rst_8) resets DMA PMP state without resetting the DMA wrapper's configuration registers, enabling reuse of stale transfer parameters with a reset PMP permission state.

evidence.description: The dma_wrapper has reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs for register lock and access control, but the DMA sub-module instantiation (line 191+) does not show these signals being gated on the rst_8 reset path or on the DMA permission checks. The core_lock_reg signal (line 54) is declared but its enforcement relative to PMP checks is not visible in the snippet.
evidence.supports_claim: Access control signals exist in the wrapper but may not protect against the rst_8-induced PMP state reset vulnerability.

evidence.description: After reset, pmp_allow_reg = 0 (deny) and pmp_check_ctr_reg = 0. If rst_8 resets the DMA mid-transfer, pmp_check_ctr_reg resets to 0, which could bypass the iterative PMP check loop (which counts down checks for address ranges). If the counter is 0, the DMA may proceed to the store/load phase without completing pending PMP checks.
evidence.supports_claim: Resetting pmp_check_ctr_reg to 0 via rst_8 during an active DMA operation could bypass in-progress PMP address range checks.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain centers on pmp_access_type_reg in the DMA module, which is part of a PMP (Physical Memory Protection) permission checking mechanism. Two concrete issues are found in the source snippets: (1) Blocking assignments are used in an always_ff block for security-critical PMP registers during reset, which is an RTL coding violation that can cause simulation/synthesis divergence. (2) The DMA is instantiated with 'rst_ni && ~rst_8', so a separate reset signal (rst_8) can independently clear the DMA's PMP check state (pmp_check_ctr_reg, pmp_allow_reg, pmp_access_type_reg) while the wrapper retains the previously programmed DMA transfer parameters. This desynchronization means that after rst_8, the DMA could start a new transfer using stale addresses with a freshly-reset (and thus unchecked) PMP permission state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or privileged software with access to the rst_8 signal could reset the DMA's PMP permission tracking mid-transfer or before a new transfer, potentially bypassing physical memory protection checks and allowing DMA transfers to or from protected memory regions.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full dma_wrapper.sv source is not provided, so it is unclear exactly how core_lock_reg, reglk_ctrl_i, and acct_ctrl_i interact with the DMA instantiation and whether they provide compensating controls. The exact semantics of rst_8 (who controls it and under what conditions) are also not shown. The PMP check FSM logic after line 166 in dma.sv is only partially visible (SNIP-0005 shows some of it), so the full bypass path cannot be confirmed with certainty.

finding.summary: The DMA module's pmp_addr_reg and related PMP security registers use blocking assignments (=) inside a clocked always block during reset, which is a coding style violation that can cause non-deterministic simulation/synthesis behavior. More critically, the reset path for the DMA module uses a combined expression (rst_ni && ~rst_8) that bypasses the standard reset signal, and PMP security state registers in dma.sv are initialized with blocking assignments in the reset clause of a clocked always_ff block, potentially causing synthesis-simulation mismatches.

evidence.description: In the reg_update always block (line 138-164), during reset (!rst_ni), all PMP-related registers including pmp_addr_reg, pmp_access_type_reg, pmp_allow_reg, pmp_check_ctr_reg, and valid_reg are assigned using blocking assignments (=) instead of non-blocking assignments (<=). This is a serious coding error inside a clocked sequential block that can cause simulation-synthesis mismatches, potentially leaving security registers in unpredictable states after reset.
evidence.supports_claim: Lines 142-147 show blocking assignments (=) used inside a clocked always @ (posedge clk_i or negedge rst_ni) block for security-critical PMP registers: dma_ctrl_reg = 'h0; pmp_addr_reg = 64'h0; pmp_access_type_reg = riscv::ACCESS_READ; pmp_allow_reg = 'h0; pmp_check_ctr_reg = 'h0; valid_reg = 'h0;

evidence.description: The DMA module is instantiated with rst_ni = (rst_ni && ~rst_8), meaning rst_8 can independently reset the DMA. If rst_8 is asserted while rst_ni is high, the DMA including its PMP state gets reset, potentially clearing PMP protection mid-operation without a full system reset, which could be exploitable to bypass access controls.
evidence.supports_claim: Line 196: .rst_ni (rst_ni && ~rst_8) shows an additional reset path through rst_8 that independently clears all DMA PMP security state registers.

evidence.description: The pmp_allow_reg is initialized to 0 (deny) on reset, which appears correct. However, due to the blocking assignment in the reset clause of a clocked sequential block, the actual synthesized behavior may differ from simulation, potentially leaving pmp_allow_reg in an unintended state after reset.
evidence.supports_claim: pmp_allow_reg = 'h0 at line 145 uses blocking assignment in sequential always block, creating synthesis-simulation mismatch risk for the PMP allow register that gatekeeps DMA memory access permissions.

evidence.description: The dma_wrapper accepts reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i inputs for register lock and access control, but the rst_8 input (line 73) is declared as a regular input with no privilege or authentication check shown in the available code, meaning any agent able to assert rst_8 can reset DMA PMP state.

This mixed usage is a well-known RTL coding error that causes synthesis-simulation mismatches, meaning post-synthesis behavior of PMP state after reset may differ from simulation, potentially bypassing PMP protections. (2) The DMA reset is driven by (rst_ni && ~rst_8), where rst_8 is an unguarded input that can independently reset all DMA PMP security state without a full system reset, potentially allowing an attacker who can control rst_8 to clear PMP state mid-transaction.

Additionally, synthesis-simulation mismatch from blocking assignments in sequential reset logic may leave PMP security registers in undefined post-reset states in silicon, undermining DMA memory isolation guarantees.

finding.summary: The DMA PMP permission check can be bypassed via a hardcoded `we_flag` shortcut in the CTRL_CHECK_LOAD state, allowing DMA transfers to proceed regardless of PMP access violations when `we_flag` is asserted and `length_d == 3`.

evidence.description: At line 216, the expression `pmp_allow_new = (pmp_allow_reg && (pmp_data_allow || (pmp_check_ctr_reg == length_d))) || (we_flag && length_d == 3)` contains a bypass: when `we_flag` is asserted AND `length_d == 3`, `pmp_allow_new` is forced to 1 regardless of the actual PMP check result (`pmp_data_allow`). evidence.description: The `pmp_allow_reg` register accumulates per-chunk PMP check results and is ultimately used at CTRL_CHECK_LOAD completion to decide whether the DMA load (CTRL_LOAD) is permitted. Forcing it to 1 via the we_flag shortcut corrupts this security gate.

evidence.description: At line 207, the transition to CTRL_LOAD (actual DMA read) requires `pmp_allow_reg && pmp_data_allow`. evidence.supports_claim: The guard at line 207 is insufficient because the accumulated pmp_allow_reg can be maliciously set to 1 through the we_flag bypass path.

evidence.description: CTRL_LOAD and CTRL_STORE states trigger actual AXI DMA memory read and write operations. Access to these states is gated by PMP checks; bypassing the PMP check allows DMA to read from or write to memory regions that should be protected.

finding.reasoning_summary: The DMA module's CTRL_CHECK_LOAD state iterates through source address chunks and accumulates PMP permission results into `pmp_allow_reg`. At line 216, the update expression for `pmp_allow_new` includes an OR branch `(we_flag && length_d == 3)` that unconditionally sets the permission accumulator to 1 regardless of what `pmp_data_allow` reports. When this branch fires, the subsequent check at line 207 (`pmp_allow_reg && pmp_data_allow`) can still pass (pmp_data_allow is checked again at terminal iteration), but the accumulator has been corrupted. More critically, even if pmp_data_allow happens to be 0 at the final check iteration while pmp_allow_reg was forced to 1, the combined `pmp_allow_reg && pmp_data_allow` would be 0 — however, the bypass means that for all intermediate iterations the allow register accumulates as 1 even for non-allowed addresses, which undermines the integrity of the entire PMP walk. Furthermore, `we_flag` is a software-driven input with no hardware enforcement, making this a software-exploitable PMP bypass.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert `we_flag` and set `length_d == 3` can bypass PMP access checks for DMA source address ranges, potentially enabling unauthorized DMA reads from PMP-protected memory regions, which could lead to information disclosure or privilege escalation.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level required to assert we_flag is not fully visible from available snippets. The riscv_peripherals instantiation shows acct_ctrl_i as an account control input, but it is unclear whether it gates we_flag or only other accesses. evidence.description: dma_wrapper declares reglk_ctrl_i (register lock values) and acct_ctrl_i (access control) as inputs, and also has core_lock_reg as an internal signal. These signals exist as the intended permission/lock mechanism for protecting DMA configuration registers including length_reg.

evidence.description: In dma.sv, the PMP check for load (CTRL_CHECK_LOAD) contains the expression: pmp_allow_new = (pmp_allow_reg && (pmp_data_allow || (pmp_check_ctr_reg == length_d))) || (we_flag && length_d == 3). The term '(we_flag && length_d == 3)' unconditionally sets pmp_allow_new=1 whenever we_flag is asserted and length_d equals 3, bypassing the PMP check result (pmp_data_allow) entirely. This is a guard bypass in the PMP permission logic.
evidence.supports_claim: The PMP permission check can be bypassed by setting we_flag=1 and length=3, regardless of actual PMP policy.

evidence.description: length_i is a direct input to the dma module that feeds into PMP check counter initialization (pmp_check_ctr_new = length_d) and into the bypass condition (length_d == 3). The length_reg from dma_wrapper drives length_i.
evidence.supports_claim: length_reg is a security-critical parameter that controls the PMP bypass condition.

evidence.description: The dma module is instantiated with rst_ni = (rst_ni && ~rst_8), meaning asserting rst_8 forces a reset of the DMA controller (clearing dma_ctrl_reg, pmp_allow_reg, etc.) but does not clear the configuration registers (length_reg, source/dest addr regs) in dma_wrapper. After rst_8 de-asserts, the DMA resumes from a reset state but uses whatever values remain in the wrapper's registers, potentially re-triggering a transfer with stale or attacker-controlled parameters.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset via rst_8 resets the DMA FSM state but not the configuration registers, creating an inconsistent security state.

evidence.description: In dma.sv save_inputs block, configuration registers (length_d, source/dest addresses) are updated whenever dma_ctrl_reg == CTRL_IDLE || CTRL_DONE, i.e., whenever the DMA is not actively transferring. There is no check of any permission/lock signal before latching new values from length_i into length_d, meaning any write that reaches length_reg in the wrapper is immediately used.
evidence.supports_claim: No permission check gates the latching of length_i into the active DMA parameter register length_d.

evidence.description: Because length_d == 3 combined with we_flag bypasses PMP enforcement (pmp_allow_new forced to 1), an attacker who can write length_reg=3 via the AXI interface can cause the DMA to skip PMP read-permission checks and transfer data from any source address, constituting a privilege escalation / memory isolation bypass.
evidence.supports_claim: Exploiting the bypass allows DMA reads from PMP-protected memory regions.

finding.reasoning_summary: The DMA wrapper exposes length_reg and other configuration registers over AXI-Lite. The dma.sv PMP check logic in CTRL_CHECK_LOAD contains an explicit bypass: if we_flag is asserted and length_d equals 3, pmp_allow_new is forced to 1 regardless of pmp_data_allow, entirely defeating the PMP read-permission check. Since length_reg in dma_wrapper feeds length_i -> length_d in dma.sv with no lock/permission guard on the write path, an entity that can write length_reg=3 and assert we_flag can bypass PMP enforcement. Additionally, the rst_8 partial reset clears the FSM but not configuration registers, leaving a security gap after reset.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI-Lite write access to the DMA registers can write length_reg=3 and assert we_flag to bypass PMP read checks, enabling the DMA to read from arbitrarily protected memory regions and exfiltrate data. This represents a complete bypass of memory protection for DMA transfers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet showing the register-write path in dma_wrapper (where length_reg is actually written and where reglk_ctrl_i/core_lock_reg are checked or not checked) is not fully shown, so the completeness of the lock guard on writes to length_reg is inferred from what is visible. However, the bypass in pmp_allow_new computation in SNIP-0006 is explicit and unambiguous in the source.

raw_llm_analysis.no_finding_reason: The source snippets show a well-formed permission signal propagation path. The reglk_ctrl signal is declared in riscv_peripherals (SNIP-0001), a specific slice reglk_ctrl[8*8+7:8*8] is correctly passed to i_dma_wrapper (SNIP-0002), and dma_wrapper receives it as reglk_ctrl_i input (SNIP-0003). There is no evidence in the provided snippets of a missing guard, bypassed lock check, unsafe reset behavior, sensitive readback, or incorrect protection state update. The chain demonstrates a normal, connected permission lock signal wiring with no observable violation.

finding.summary: When NR_ENTRIES is zero, the PMP module unconditionally grants all memory accesses regardless of privilege level, address, or access type, effectively bypassing all PMP enforcement.

evidence.description: Line 69: `else assign allow_o = 1'b1;` — when the parameter NR_ENTRIES equals 0, the entire PMP checking logic (the `gen_pmp` generate block) is skipped and allow_o is permanently driven high, meaning every access from every privilege level is unconditionally permitted with no checks whatsoever.
evidence.supports_claim: With zero PMP entries configured, the module provides no memory protection at all; S-mode and U-mode accesses are granted the same as M-mode with no enforcement of any access restrictions.

evidence.description: Lines 47–67 contain the entire access-control decision logic gated inside `if (NR_ENTRIES > 0) begin : gen_pmp`. This block checks privilege level, match results, locked bits, and access-type permissions — but all of this is absent when NR_ENTRIES == 0.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege-level check (`priv_lvl_i != riscv::PRIV_LVL_M || conf_i[i].locked`) and access-type check (`(access_type_i & conf_i[i].access_type) != access_type_i`) only exist inside the generate block and are entirely absent for the zero-entry case.

evidence.description: The RISC-V PMP specification requires that when no PMP entries are implemented, M-mode has unrestricted access but S-mode and U-mode have no access to any region (all accesses fail). The implementation inverts this: it grants all accesses (`allow_o = 1'b1`) unconditionally for all privilege levels when NR_ENTRIES == 0.
evidence.supports_claim: Granting allow_o=1 unconditionally for S/U modes when there are no PMP entries violates the RISC-V spec requirement that S/U mode accesses must fail when no PMP entry matches.

evidence.description: The pmp_entry module computes address match results (match_o) for individual PMP regions. When NR_ENTRIES==0 no pmp_entry instances are created, so no address-range enforcement is applied and the match-based gate in the parent pmp module is never consulted.
evidence.supports_claim: Absence of any pmp_entry instances when NR_ENTRIES==0 means the entire region-matching mechanism is bypassed, confirming the unconditional allow_o=1 path is the only path active.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0001 line 69 shows that when NR_ENTRIES==0, allow_o is driven to constant 1 via a continuous assignment outside the generate block. The RISC-V privileged specification states that when no PMP entries are present, M-mode retains full access but S-mode and U-mode must have all accesses denied (access fault). The RTL implementation does the opposite by granting all privilege levels unconditional access. This is a concrete permission bypass: S-mode and U-mode software can access any physical address without restriction when the PMP is instantiated with zero entries.
finding.security_impact: Any software running in S-mode or U-mode can read, write, or execute any physical memory region without restriction when NR_ENTRIES==0. This completely defeats the PMP's purpose of isolating unprivileged software from privileged memory regions, potentially enabling privilege escalation, confidential data exfiltration, or control-flow hijacking by unprivileged code.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The RISC-V privileged specification text is not included as a snippet, so the claim about spec-required behavior for zero PMP entries relies on well-known RISC-V ISA knowledge rather than an in-context artifact. However, the RTL behavior itself (unconditional allow_o=1 for all privilege levels) is directly visible in SNIP-0001 line 69 with no ambiguity.

finding.summary: AXI protection bits (aw_prot/ar_prot) are hardwired to zero for all AXI-lite peripherals (PLIC, RST, RSA, RNG, AES0), permanently disabling privilege-level and secure/non-secure transaction signaling on every bus transaction.

evidence.description: plic_master.aw_prot and ar_prot are tied to '0, meaning all PLIC transactions carry unprivileged, non-secure, data-access protection attributes regardless of the actual privilege level of the initiating software.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI PROT field is permanently cleared, bypassing privilege signaling for PLIC accesses.

evidence.description: rst_axi_req.aw.prot and rst_axi_req.ar.prot are both assigned '0 unconditionally for the RST peripheral, removing any privilege or secure/non-secure distinction from those bus transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI PROT field is permanently cleared for RST peripheral accesses, bypassing privilege signaling.

evidence.description: rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot are both tied to '0 for the RSA wrapper, which handles cryptographic operations. This prevents the interconnect or downstream protection checkers from distinguishing privileged vs. unprivileged or secure vs. non-secure accesses to RSA registers.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI PROT field is permanently cleared for RSA peripheral accesses, bypassing privilege and security signaling on a cryptographic peripheral.

evidence.description: rng_axi_req.aw.prot and rng_axi_req.ar.prot are both tied to '0 for the RNG peripheral, removing privilege-level and secure/non-secure information from all RNG bus transactions.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI PROT field is permanently cleared for RNG peripheral accesses, bypassing privilege signaling on an entropy source peripheral.

evidence.description: aes0_axi_req.aw.prot and aes0_axi_req.ar.prot are tied to '0 for the AES0 peripheral. Cryptographic key/data registers accessible via this interface will appear to the interconnect as unprivileged non-secure accesses regardless of the actual caller's privilege.
evidence.supports_claim: AXI PROT field is permanently cleared for AES peripheral accesses, bypassing privilege and security signaling on a cryptographic peripheral.

evidence.description: DMA wrapper instantiation shows that reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl are wired, indicating the design does attempt permission control in some peripherals, making the absence of prot signaling elsewhere a notable inconsistency.
evidence.supports_claim: Other peripherals receive access-control signals; the prot=0 tie-off on AXI channels is not an intentional global policy.

evidence.description: PLIC master interface is used to arbitrate interrupt priorities and enable registers for all system peripherals. Hardwired prot=0 means interconnect-level protection schemes cannot distinguish M-mode from U-mode accesses to PLIC configuration registers.
evidence.supports_claim: PLIC configuration registers are a security-sensitive resource whose access should carry correct privilege attributes.

finding.reasoning_summary: Across all AXI-lite peripheral adapters (PLIC, RST, RSA, RNG, AES0), the AXI AxPROT field is unconditionally driven to zero. AMBA AXI PROT[0] signals unprivileged vs. privileged access, PROT[1] signals secure vs. non-secure, and PROT[2] signals instruction vs. data. By forcing all three bits to zero, the RTL presents every transaction as an unprivileged, non-secure, data access. Any downstream interconnect firewall or memory protection unit that relies on PROT to enforce privilege boundaries will see incorrect (too-permissive) attribute values, effectively bypassing privilege-level access control for security-critical peripherals including the RSA accelerator and AES engine. The DMA instance in the same module does wire reglk_ctrl and acct_ctrl, confirming the design intent is to enforce access control; the PROT tie-off undermines that intent at the bus level.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged software context could initiate or influence accesses to cryptographic (RSA, AES) and entropy (RNG) peripherals and the PLIC without the interconnect being able to apply privilege-based firewall rules, because the AXI PROT attributes always signal unprivileged non-secure access. This can lead to unauthorized reads of cryptographic keys, unauthorized reprogramming of interrupt priorities, or corruption of RNG state.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether the SoC interconnect actually uses AXI PROT for enforcement is not shown in the provided snippets. If the interconnect ignores PROT entirely, the impact is limited to missing defense-in-depth. Additionally, the AXI-lite shim wrapper that drives plic_master may intentionally strip PROT; the source of the master-side AxPROT values from the CPU is not visible in the provided code, so it is possible the shim is the intended authority for PROT and the CPU-supplied value is discarded by design.

finding.summary: Commented-out access control guard for bootrom access leaves a previously disabled privilege check active, while the DMA enable path depends on a single-bit access control that may be insufficiently enforced, revealing an incomplete privilege enforcement model.

evidence.description: Line 516 shows a commented-out assignment '//assign rom_req = rom_req_acct;' that would pass ROM requests unconditionally. The active assignment on line 517 'assign rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0];' gates bootrom access on acc_ctrl_c. The comment directly above indicates the guard was previously bypassed (the unguarded form was the prior active code), demonstrating the guard was not always enforced and may still be toggled by developers, creating fragile security posture.
evidence.supports_claim: The commented-out line immediately above the active guard shows the ROM access control was intentionally removable/bypassable, and the active guard is a single continuous assignment with no lock or lifecycle protection.

evidence.description: DMA enable is computed as 'assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i;' — a single combinational AND of an AXI-derived enable and the access control input. There is no registered, locked, or lifecycle-aware protection; a glitch or incorrect acc_ctrl value directly enables DMA.
evidence.supports_claim: The DMA access enable is purely combinational with no hardening against transient or incorrect access control values.

evidence.description: The DMA wrapper is instantiated with 'acct_ctrl_i ( acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8])' — a single bit from the restructured access control matrix indexed by runtime privilege level. The privilege level directly indexes the access control array with no bounds checking or sanitization visible in this chain.
evidence.supports_claim: Runtime privilege level directly selects the access control bit passed to DMA without intermediate validation.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is computed as 'acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i])' — peripheral 5's access bits are ORed with peripheral 4's access bits. This means granting access to peripheral 4 also grants access to peripheral 5, creating an implicit privilege escalation path between peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The OR coupling of peripheral 4 and peripheral 5 access control bits means a less-sensitive peripheral's permissions can inadvertently enable a more sensitive one.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain reveals two concrete permission issues: (1) The bootrom access guard (SNIP-0003) has a commented-out bypass immediately above the active guard, showing the check is fragile and was previously disabled — this is a documented pattern of bypassed access control. (2) The acc_ctrl_c computation (SNIP-0001) ORs peripheral 5's access bits with peripheral 4's, meaning access granted to peripheral 4 automatically grants access to peripheral 5, which is an implicit privilege escalation. The DMA enable (SNIP-0007) is a purely combinational single-bit check with no hardening. Together these represent an incomplete and fragile access control model.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or misconfigured software with access to peripheral 4 controls gains unintended access to peripheral 5. The commented-out ROM bypass could be re-enabled by a developer, removing bootrom access protection. The combinational DMA enable provides no defense against glitching or transient access control misconfiguration, potentially allowing unauthorized DMA transfers.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full definition of acc_ctrl register (how it is written, whether it is locked by reglk_ctrl) is not in scope. The identity of peripheral 5 vs peripheral 4 is not confirmed from snippets alone. The impact of the commented-out ROM guard depends on development/deployment practices. Privilege level sanitization upstream is not visible in this chain.

finding.summary: DMA register readback exposes sensitive configuration (source/dest addresses, core_lock, start, length) to any privilege level when 'en' is asserted, and 'en' itself is gated only by acct_ctrl_i which is derived from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8]. However, the read path in dma_wrapper allows all internal DMA registers to be read back whenever 'en' is true, with no additional privilege or lock check on the read side beyond what gates 'en'. More critically, the commented-out line in riscv_peripherals.sv (line 516) shows the ROM access control guard was previously disabled and then re-enabled, indicating a history of access control bypass. The DMA read path (lines 160-185) exposes core_lock_reg, source/dest addresses, and other sensitive DMA state whenever 'en' is asserted, with no differentiation between read and write permission levels.

evidence.description: The 'en' signal is formed as 'en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i', gating DMA access on the access control input. This is the sole permission gate for both reads and writes to DMA registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The only access control applied to DMA register access is acct_ctrl_i; there is no separate privilege check for read vs. write operations.

evidence.description: The read path (lines 157-186) returns all DMA register contents including core_lock_reg, source_addr_lsb/msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb/msb_reg, start_reg, length_reg, done_reg, end_reg, and valid whenever 'en' is asserted, with no additional privilege check or lock guard on the read side. Any software that passes the acct_ctrl_i gate can read all sensitive DMA configuration registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive DMA registers (addresses, control, lock state) are readable without any additional privilege or lock enforcement beyond the single 'en' gate.

evidence.description: Line 516 shows a commented-out version 'assign rom_req = rom_req_acct;' that bypasses the access control check entirely. This demonstrates a pattern where access control guards can be disabled, and indicates the access control enforcement is fragile and has been bypassed in the past.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control check for ROM (and by analogy DMA) can be commented out, leaving resources accessible without privilege validation.

evidence.description: The DMA wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i = acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8], which is a privilege-level-indexed access control bit. This is the permission token passed to dma_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: Access control is indexed by priv_lvl_i; however this single bit controls both read and write access, with no differentiation.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is computed as a remapping of acc_ctrl bits, with special OR logic for index 5. This is the source of the access control values distributed to peripherals including the DMA.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control value reaching the DMA is derived from a shared register with specific bit indexing, making it a coarse-grained single-bit gate for all DMA register operations.

evidence.description: DMA internal registers including source_addr_lsb_reg, source_addr_msb_reg, dest_addr_lsb_reg, dest_addr_msb_reg, core_lock_reg, start_reg, length_reg are all readable back through the rdata path with no lock or privilege differentiation.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive DMA transfer parameters and lock state are exposed as readable resources protected only by a single access control bit.

finding.reasoning_summary: The DMA wrapper uses a single 'en' signal (gated by acct_ctrl_i AND en_acct) to control both read and write access to all DMA configuration registers. The read path (always @(*) block, lines 157-186) returns all sensitive register contents including transfer source/destination addresses, core lock state, and control registers whenever 'en' is true, with no additional privilege check or lock enforcement on the read side. The access control is a single bit per privilege level, providing no differentiation between read and write permissions, and no protection for sensitive address readback. Additionally, the commented-out line in riscv_peripherals.sv (line 516) demonstrates that access control guards in this codebase have been and can be disabled entirely. The combination of coarse-grained access control, no read-specific guards, and a demonstrated pattern of disabling access control checks constitutes a permission-related vulnerability.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or lower-privilege software entity that satisfies the acct_ctrl_i gate can read back sensitive DMA transfer addresses (source and destination), transfer length, start triggers, and the core lock register. This enables information disclosure of memory layout and ongoing DMA operations. Additionally, the fragility of the access control (single bit, historically bypassed) means the guard itself may be disabled, allowing unrestricted DMA register access including writes to source/dest addresses, potentially enabling DMA-based memory corruption.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege level mapping and whether acct_ctrl_i effectively restricts unprivileged access depends on the acc_ctrl register initialization and programming, which is not shown in the snippets. It is also unclear whether there are higher-level bus access controls that further restrict who can reach the DMA AXI interface. The read-back concern is structural but the actual exploitability depends on the threat model and privilege separation in the full system.

raw_llm_analysis.no_finding_reason: The source snippet (SNIP-0001, lines 1779-1798) shows only the tie-off of AXI-lite unused fields (id, len, burst, lock, cache, prot, qos, region, atop, w.last) to constant zero or fixed values. Notably, the aw.prot and ar.prot fields are set to '0 (unprivileged, non-secure, data access), which is common for peripheral bus adapters. There is no evidence of a missing guard, bypassed lock or authentication check, unsafe debug behavior, sensitive readback, or incorrect protection state update within this chain. The chain context does not contain any permission or security control logic—only constant assignments for unused bus fields.

finding.summary: AXI protection bits (aw.prot and ar.prot) for the AES0 peripheral are permanently tied to zero, disabling all AXI transaction-level privilege, security, and instruction/data type signaling for AES key material access.

evidence.description: Lines 1250 and 1261 permanently assign aes0_axi_req.aw.prot = '0 and aes0_axi_req.ar.prot = '0. The ARPROT/AWPROT fields encode privilege level (bit 0), security state (bit 1), and instruction/data type (bit 2). Tying them to zero means every AES register access is presented to the interconnect as an unprivileged, non-secure, data access, stripping any downstream bus-protection filtering that relies on these bits to allow only secure/privileged masters to reach the AES peripheral.
evidence.supports_claim: The aw.prot and ar.prot fields are hardwired to 0, so the AXI interconnect and any downstream protection monitors/firewalls cannot distinguish unprivileged/non-secure bus transactions from legitimate privileged/secure ones targeting the AES engine, effectively bypassing AXI-level access control for cryptographic key and data registers.

evidence.description: The aes0_axi_req bus drives the AES0 wrapper (aes0_wrapper), which is a cryptographic accelerator whose registers hold secret key material and plaintext/ciphertext. The AXI request signal is constructed with no privilege or security qualification.
evidence.supports_claim: The resource being accessed without proper AXI protection signaling is an AES cryptographic engine, making the missing protection bits security-relevant.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet shows that the AXI-lite to full-AXI bridge shim for AES0 statically ties aes0_axi_req.aw.prot and aes0_axi_req.ar.prot to all-zeros. In the AXI specification, PROT[1] = 0 means non-secure and PROT[0] = 0 means unprivileged. Any interconnect firewall or TrustZone-aware component that gates access to the AES peripheral based on these bits will see every transaction as unprivileged and non-secure, regardless of the actual privilege/security level of the initiating software. This is a static bypass of AXI transaction-level access control for the AES cryptographic peripheral.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or non-secure software agent whose bus transactions are forwarded through this shim will have its transactions presented to the AES peripheral (and any intervening interconnect filter) with exactly the same protection attributes as a legitimate secure/privileged master. If a downstream firewall or memory protection unit relies on AWPROT/ARPROT to deny non-secure or unprivileged access to AES registers, that protection is silently bypassed, potentially allowing leakage of AES keys or unauthorized encryption/decryption operations.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain context does not include the AES wrapper instantiation, the interconnect firewall configuration, or any downstream protection-checking logic that would confirm whether PROT bits are actually evaluated. If no downstream component checks PROT, the impact is lower. Conversely, if TrustZone or a bus firewall does check these bits, the impact is high. The finding is based solely on the static zero-assignment visible in the snippet.

finding.summary: AXI protection (PROT) and lock fields for AES1 AXI requests are permanently hardwired to zero, disabling all AXI bus-level access control and exclusive-access signaling for the AES peripheral.

evidence.description: Lines 1336 and 1347 permanently assign aes1_axi_req.aw.prot and aes1_axi_req.ar.prot to '0 (unprivileged, non-secure, data access). Lines 1334 and 1345 permanently assign aw.lock and ar.lock to '0, disabling exclusive-access signaling. These fields are tied off unconditionally with no runtime configurability or privilege gating.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI PROT field being hardwired to zero means all AES1 transactions are issued as unprivileged, non-secure data accesses regardless of the actual privilege level of the initiating software. Any downstream interconnect or memory protection unit that relies on PROT to enforce access control cannot distinguish between legitimate privileged AES operations and unprivileged accesses, effectively bypassing bus-level permission enforcement for the AES crypto peripheral.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet shows that aes1_axi_req.aw.prot and aes1_axi_req.ar.prot are statically assigned to '0 for all AES1 AXI transactions. In the AXI protocol, the PROT field encodes privilege level (bit 0), security domain (bit 1), and instruction/data type (bit 2). Hardwiring these to zero signals unprivileged, non-secure, data access on every request. If an interconnect fabric or a memory protection unit uses PROT to gate access to sensitive AES key/data registers, this tie-off causes all requests to be mislabeled, potentially allowing unprivileged bus masters to drive or observe AES transactions indistinguishably from privileged ones, and preventing any protection infrastructure from enforcing privilege separation on the AES peripheral bus interface.
finding.security_impact: An AXI interconnect or peripheral protection controller relying on the PROT field to enforce privilege- or security-domain-based access restrictions for the AES crypto block will receive incorrect metadata on every transaction. This can allow unprivileged or non-secure access to AES key material and configuration registers, undermining the confidentiality and integrity guarantees of the cryptographic subsystem. The impact is proportional to whether downstream components enforce PROT-based filtering; in systems that do, the hardwired zero constitutes a complete bypass of bus-level access control for AES1.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual downstream interconnect and memory protection unit configurations are not present in the provided context, so it cannot be confirmed whether any component actively enforces PROT-based filtering. If the downstream fabric does not use PROT for access control, the practical impact is reduced. Additionally, the comment 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite' suggests this may be intentional for an AXI-lite bridging scenario, but the security implication of permanently zeroing PROT remains regardless of design intent.

finding.summary: AES2 AXI request protection signal (aw.prot and ar.prot) is hardcoded to zero, disabling AXI protection signaling for the AES peripheral

evidence.description: Both aes2_axi_req.aw.prot and aes2_axi_req.ar.prot are permanently tied to '0 (lines 1421 and 1432). The AXI PROT signal encodes privilege level, security level, and instruction/data access type. Hardcoding to zero means the AES peripheral always appears to be accessed in unprivileged, non-secure, data mode, which can bypass downstream protection checks in AXI interconnects or slave peripherals that rely on PROT to enforce access control.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI protection attribute for AES2 transactions is permanently forced to zero, removing any privilege or security-level enforcement that downstream bus components might apply based on the PROT field.

evidence.description: The aes2_axi_req bus carries AXI write-address (aw) and read-address (ar) channels to the AES2 cryptographic peripheral. The aw.lock and ar.lock signals are also tied to zero (lines 1419, 1430), disabling exclusive-access signaling, further reducing access control signaling fidelity.
evidence.supports_claim: All AXI security and privilege attributes for the AES2 peripheral are statically suppressed, meaning the AES hardware is reachable by any bus master without privilege or security level filtering.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet shows that for the AES2 AXI interface, aw.prot and ar.prot are both tied to '0. In the AXI protocol, the PROT field carries three bits: privileged access, non-secure access, and instruction/data access. By hardcoding this to zero (unprivileged, secure, data), any AXI interconnect or memory protection unit that gates access based on PROT can no longer distinguish between privileged and unprivileged accesses to the AES peripheral. Additionally, aw.lock and ar.lock are tied to zero, disabling exclusive access signaling. This represents a missing guard: the design should propagate actual privilege and security-level signals from the initiating master rather than permanently suppressing them.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged or less-trusted master could potentially reach the AES2 cryptographic peripheral through the AXI fabric if any downstream firewall relies on PROT bits to enforce access control. This could enable unauthorized reads or writes to AES key material, configuration, or output registers, undermining the confidentiality and integrity guarantees of the cryptographic accelerator.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual downstream AXI interconnect and slave protection checking logic is not present in the provided snippets. If no downstream component enforces PROT-based filtering, the practical exploitability is reduced. The severity also depends on whether the AES peripheral contains key registers directly accessible via AXI or whether key loading is gated by other means. The chain context does not include the AES2 slave logic or interconnect firewall configuration.

raw_llm_analysis.no_finding_reason: The source snippet (SNIP-0001, lines 657-692) shows standard AXI-lite tie-off assignments for the CLINT AXI request bus. Fields not used by AXI-lite (id, len, burst, lock, cache, qos, region, atop) are correctly zeroed out, and size is set to 3'b11 for 8-byte transfers. Notably, the AXI protection field (aw.prot and ar.prot) is assigned zero (unprivileged, unsecure, data access), which is a common and intentional default for internal peripheral bus wiring in this class of open-source RISC-V SoC. There is no missing guard, bypassed lock or authentication check, unsafe reset/debug behavior, sensitive readback, or incorrect protection state update visible in this chain.
